sentences.text: Yes.
It's going to be a very interesting session, I think.
I feel very privileged to have this opportunity to hopefully remember what happened so many years ago.
Certainly.
I'm Dorothea R-- middle initial-- Bloch.
Dorothea, D-O-R-O-T-H-E-A.
My middle initial is Rica, R-I-C-A, and Bloch, B-L-O-C-H.
My name at birth was Dorothea Rica Wallach, W-A-L-L-A-C-H.
I was called Thea in Germany.
That was not a nickname, it was just short for Dorothea.
My birth date?
I was born January 16, 1924 in Hamburg, in the Jewish hospital in Hamburg, one of 91 babies born there in 1924.
And I'm now 71 years old.
Excuse me.
Hamburg, H-A-M-B-U-R-G.
That is in northern Germany, the second largest city in Germany, and a very large port.
What-- I will try to remember as best as I can from my earlier years.
I, I think I can remember, maybe, back to perhaps three years of age when I was a flower girl at my mother's youngest brother's wedding.
And the one thing I remember about it is that I spilled the flowers out too quickly, and then hollered out loud that all my flowers are gone.
What should I do?
And there I was and that's, that's about, I think, the earliest I can remember from my really earlier years.
[CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me.
I remember taking walks with my mother in the fall when the big chestnut trees would drop their chestnuts, and getting a big kick out of picking up the chestnuts.
And my mother used to take me wherever.
We need-- we went for walks quite frequently.
I had a lot of little friends.
We went tobogganing in one of the nice parks in Hamburg.
And-- then I remember when we moved from a smaller place to a little larger place.
And I had just received a new, a new doll buggy.
And we walked-- the moving was done by whatever truck, I don't recall that-- but my mother and I walked from the one place where we lived to the new place.
And I was just practically jumping with joy behind that new buggy and the doll was tucked in under the cover.
And when we arrived at this new apartment, which was 24 [GERMAN] in Hamburg, I, I, I experienced what was probably my very first encounter with anti-Semitism.
My mother, when she approached the entrance, turned all shades of white.
I mean, she just got as white as a sheet.

And she had seen where our name had been newly fastened to the side of the entrance, where all the names were etched, that somebody had taken a, a crayon or whatever-- it was black-- and painted swastikas over our name.
And that was truly, I guess, my very first-- this was in 1930-- and that was my very first encounter with anti-Semitism.
And we actually lived in that apartment for about four years.
And we-- I did find some very nice little friends there, and I had a fairly normal life.
There-
At that time, I was five years old.
And I entered school at six, and walked from there to my school in the Kielort Allee, which was the grade school from first through fourth grade.
Can you spell the name of that school?
Yes.
K-I-E-L-O-R-T and Allee is like-- A-L-L-E-E.
That's actually a French word, but it's named-- it's used in Germany frequently for street.
Instead of street, it's allee, like avenue, something like that.
And that was the name of the school.
It was called by the name of the street where it was located.

And what I would like to bring out is the fact that we eventually had to move from there after four years, but we had some very wonderful neighbors, Gentile people-- the Paulsons and the Alrichs and I can't remember one other name in particular.

And we had, in that street, and the streets-- the other streets that ran parallel for it, to it-- excuse me-- were actually a-- almost a-- you could almost say a hot bed of early Nazis, which apparently my parents didn't know when we first moved there.
One picture I have in my mind that I'll never forget is the sea of swastika flags that were hung there for a certain occasion.
I don't know what it was.
Election, Hitler's birthday, or whatever it was.
It was a sea of the red with the white and the black swastika inside.
And while there were, of course, some people who did not fly that flag until they had to, it was an overwhelming picture.
You could walk down that street and if you saw that, it, it was a very scary thing.
And-- so anyway, while we lived there, I attended this, this school, as I mentioned, in the Kielort Allee, and-- [CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me.
It, it was a wonderful experience.
It-- I-- the four years there was very happy years in my life.
I had a lot of friends.
I had a marvelous teacher, and we-- I loved to go to school.
When I could not go because I would have a bad cold or I'd have some kind-- I was sick, I would actually cry because I did-- I would-- I preferred to be in school.
That's how much I liked it.
It was from 1930 to '34.
We had-- [CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me.
We had little, we had little crocheting and stitching circles.
And the mothers would fix some sweets for us and lemonade to drink.
And we had a lot of homework, always, so that kept us busy.
And after the homework, probably play on the street or have a friend over to play some games, depending on the season.
But we had-- we did field trips.
So we learned a lot about Hamburg and its surrounding area.
We went to the harbor.
We found out what, what the ships that came to Hamburg would, would take on for freight or what they would deliver for freight-- freighters.
And of course, there was a big, there was still a very big traffic in Atlantic crossings, because this was really before the era of the commercial flight across the Atlantic.
And so we, we would walk home together partly, for wherever we needed-- wherever our homes were.
Partly we would walk together, and then, you know, then you'd have to go on on your own and wind up walking by yourself to your own place.

But one, one thing that comes to mind that-- [CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me-- I remember is, the year before, or the months before Hitler actually came to power in 1933, things were terribly bad in Germany.
I mean, they were bad in the '20s already.
I was born into one of the worst inflations that ever existed in the entire world economy, I guess.
But the democratic government, that [?
Weinmar, ?] the democracy, was not working very well because the Germans were too deep in debt to the Allies, who had won the war.
And they just couldn't get out from under, and it got-- it went from bad to worse.
Well anyway, that's how, of course, Hitler got some of his foothold, because of the-- one of the reasons, at least-- because of the desperate economic situation.
So we would talk about an upcoming election, and we were really quite into what was going on.
There was Hindenburg, he was the old general.
And there was the Nazi party, and there were the Social Democrats-- that was a big party-- and there was a Communist Party.
And there was another party, which was the Catholic party.
So those were the big parties, and we would talk about it.

And I remember very distinctly one of my friends saying, well, you know, my-- well, I think my parents-- one would say, well, my parents will probably vote for Hitler because he has the best program, and for what they could tell at that point.
And another one would say, my parents don't trust Hitler at all.
I don't think they're going to vote for him.
So those are the kinds of conversations we had.
Very innocent, it, it, it was.
And another thing that I remember about my walk to and from school in the morning--
[CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me.
I, I had quite a walk.
And for, for lunch-- we didn't have regular lunch periods, like we do here.
First of all, we weren't in school that long, but it was a more, it was a more concentrated session, the hours that we did spend there.
And there were just little breaks in between.
And during those little breaks, you would eat your little fruit, and bread and butter that your mother would have packed for you in your little leather purse-like thing over the shoulder.
I forgot what it was called.

And-- so when I would be on my walk and walk in the morning, and it was fairly early-- I don't recall when school did actually start-- I would frequently see men in tattered clothes-- one here, one there, not a whole group together-- walking around, looking for something to eat.
And I remember approaching one or another and offering them my sandwich.
Not with any idea that I won't have anything to eat later.
I don't think I was ever hungry.
I was a child who ate pretty good.
I was a pretty sturdy child.
But this is the thing that I remember.
And the amazing thing when you talk about this today, so many years later, that you'd be afraid nowadays, or a child would be afraid.
You wouldn't ever dream of doing such a thing.
You would have to be afraid because of the way things are nowadays.
And they look gratefully accept it.
Yes, beggars.
No.
That things were hard?
Well, yes, of course.
My father never made a fantastic living.
We were not wealthy.
There were many wealthy Jews, that's-- you know, who were in business.
We were not one of them.
We were not one of those families.
My my other sense, of course-- that was a little earlier, before I even started school, when I spent four months in Berlin with my mother's oldest sister.
And my mother's older sister and her husband, who had no children, took me in while my mother had a very serious operation.
And-- so it was also one of the most wonderful times in my life.
There wasn't a place that my aunt went that she didn't take me along.
She went to, she went to play bridge.
She went to little parties.
And always, these people would have some little extra thing for me, like a little, a couple of little chocolate bars tied together, or a little cup, or whatever.
It was just wonderful.
And her neighbors were just wonderful people.
They were not Jewish.

And what I remember there is, on various occasions, the style was, in Germany-- it probably still is nowadays, perhaps not as much-- to come home at noon or one o'clock and eat your midday meal, which is your bigger meal.
And my uncle, my uncle, Robert, Robert Brandt, would come home.
And on various occasions, he would have in tow a beggar from the streets of Berlin that he had brought-- excuse me-- brought up to the apartment.
And my aunt was not very enthusiastic about it, but she sat down and, and set a place for him.
And he ate.
He had a meal.
And so, that was-- and that also shows you'd you how good these people were.
They were, they were just utterly and totally fine and good people.
Yes.
My father, my father was a-- [CLEARING THROAT] Excuse me.
My father actually sold advertising in magazines.
And my, my early religious training, well, let's say it was practically nil.
But when I was nine-- when I was 9 o'clock-- when I was nine years old, my mother enrolled me in the temple, the reform temple, in Hamburg, for instruction.
And I-- from the first-- I wasn't very enthusiastic at first.

But from the very first visit there, I was so impressed with the kindness and with the way they went about telling us, and instructing us, and teaching us what all the various things where that are in a temple-- the bima and everything else.
And we celebrated the holidays.
And I have only, only really very good memories about then.
Now, my mother-- we did not keep the Sabbath and we did not keep kosher.
So I, you know, can't talk very much about that.
I know that when my grandmother, my mother's mother, was living-- when she died in 1916, way before I was born-- that they did have a kosher home.
Then my grandfather, her father-- my mother's father-- was left with six children.
And while they weren't little anymore, they did not keep kosher anymore.
So that, that was not a very big part in my life.
German only.
Excuse me.
Well, I don't recall, I don't recall celebrating Hanukkah, the new year, and Yom Kippur.
And that's about it.
In my earlier years, until I started to go to the Jewish school, I had several, several classmates in my grade school who were Jewish, and there were also several there who were half-Jewish.
And there weren't any who were that religious or that observing in their home, because then they would have been enrolled originally, at first grade, at the Jewish, what we call here, day school.
But it's a Jewish school that teaches Hebrew from the first grade and has all Jewish teachers.
And is-- it's a whole different thing.
But academically, it's on an equal with everything else.
So those most observant Jewish-- those children from more observing Jewish homes, they would have been enrolled in that other-- in the Jewish school.
And I was not.
I was in a public school.
It was a display of unbelievable-- it was an unbelievable vision to walk through that whole street when those flags were out.
And what it reminded me of, like a premonition or whatever that was, because I knew what Nazism meant for us Jews, even though we weren't terribly observing Jews.
It reminded me of blood all over, up and down the street.
So it, it was a horrible sight.
Well, 19-- we-- yes, we moved there when I was six years old.
Well, it was, it was out there.
You, you just knew about it.
And you knew about all the other parties.
Germany was-- unfortunately, that was one of the other big problems, that there were too many political parties in Germany.
And the Nazis, the National Socialists, had become one of the major parties.
[COUGHING] It was just something that you knew.
We-- there was no television.
We didn't even have a radio, but you knew it because your parents talked about it.
And we were-- I must say, that looking back, I would think that we were quite sophisticated in knowing what was going on and being aware of those things.
And, of course, the danger that Nazism posed to us as Jews.
Yes, my mother was very worried that, you know, that they would get into-- that they would be elected.
And as it turned out to be, historically, they never actually had a majority of votes of the German people, the Nazis.
It was through their crafty, evil ways that they got in while Hindenburg was old and feeble, and they manipulated.
And he finally relented and said, yes.
Hitler could be Chancellor.
But they never-- they did not have the majority of the voters of the German people.
Which I don't know if, if that's common knowledge.
But when you hear people say, oh, yes, all the Germans, you know, they're all at fault, I can't really agree with that.
I can't really agree with that.
And I remember-- when I was younger, I remember Nazi storm troopers and communists in uniform beating each other up on the streets.
I remember that.
That's just something that came to me.
The communists had grey and red uniforms and the Nazis, the street fighters, they had the brown uniforms, the SA.
And frequently bloody.
They'd hit each other bloody, I, I remember that.
And--
We're talking about, we're talking before the-- before 1933.
After 1933, no communist dared go out with a uniform on.
But there were a lot of communists.
And also hoping for a better, for something better.
I mean, not knowing that that wasn't going to work either, but also everybody was hoping for something better, because the average man in the street had no hope at all, economically.
It was, it was just that bad.
My parents and I.
Are you talking from when I, I was hopping behind my, my little doll buggy and walking there?
It was my parents and I.
And then we move from that-- we moved from that location.
It really became impossible to, to live there.
And I, I can't tell you exactly why, or how, or what.
We moved to another location from there.
So the last four years, we lived somewhere else.
That was-- [COUGHING] Excuse me-- 1934 or '35, or late '34, until '39, when we left Hamburg for good.
All right.
Yes, the first school I went to was the Kielort Allee, which I spelled earlier, where I had my four happy years, and was-- there was no such thing as anti-Semitism.
There wasn't anything like, you're a Jew.
We don't play with you.
That came later.
It was totally, complete acceptance.
As a matter of fact, this Mr.
Rosenberg-- and that's not a Jewish Rosenberg, that's a German Rosenberg.
I remember him telling my mother at a conference-- and I guess he didn't say it quietly enough.
He said, you know, I like having Jewish kids in my class.
They catch on so quickly.
[LAUGHS] I remember him saying that, and he didn't like dumb students.
That's one thing.
He went around with his-- when somebody didn't pay attention, he went and-- that was before we had all the problems.
We can't touch a child, you know.
Nowadays, he wouldn't be able do it.
He had a ruler in his hand.
He, he went across the knuckles.
But I was one of his favorites, so I never experienced that.
And we, we must have had a very good, must have gotten or received a very good foundation there.
If you didn't learn your pieces-- your spelling, your arithmetic, if you didn't write your sentences correctly, your grammar, you did not move on to the next grade.
That was just-- you know, you had to do it.
So there was, there was hours of homework after we got home.
And that was taken seriously, and it was a must.
And we, we would take these field trips, very early on already.
And then we would have to write about it.
And that was graded, you know.
And if there was one spelling error, it wasn't accepted.
So it was kind of striving for excellence.
And that, I felt, served me very well in later years, as well.
Excuse me, may I?
Absolutely.
[DRINKS]
